data.title: 'Wilting' Bloom Energy downgraded to Sell at Redburn
data.description:
Bloom Energy (NYSE:BE) is downgraded to Sell from Neutral with a $10 price target, down from $14.50 previously, at Redburn Atlantic on Wednesday, seeing plenty of uncertainty around the company's outlook, with current valuation "pricing in an optimistic view that does not reflect the risk reality."

Redburn's Skye Landon cites concerns over Bloom's (NYSE:BE) ability to grow earnings at market expectations, forecasting lower levels of product sales amid worries over the long-term ability of fuel cells to compete with traditional generation sources for market share.

New fuel cell competition coming to market in H2 2025 could put additional pressure on Bloom's (BE) international business, the analyst says.

data.title: Nasdaq-100 surges 10% in response to the 90-day tariff pause
data.description:
The world's fifth-largest exchange-traded fund on Wednesday rallied explosively after U.S. President Donald Trump announced a 90-day pause on most reciprocal tariffs except for China. 

The Invesco QQQ ETF (NASDAQ:QQQ) rocketed as much as +10.9% to a session high of 461.31 points. 

QQQ tracks the Nasdaq-100 index (NDX) and has a market value of $259.83B. The Nasdaq-100 (NDX) for its part also jumped as much as +10.9% to a session peak of 18,953.53 points. 

Trump issued a 90-day suspension of reciprocal tariffs for over 75 countries currently negotiating trade deals. He excluded China, against which it raised the tariff rate to 125%. 

Of the 100 stocks within the QQQ ETF, nearly all but three climbed into positive territory, propelled by the news of the tariff pause. 

If QQQ manages to maintain its 10% rally, it will set a record for the best single-day trading performance for the fund since the Great Financial Crisis. 

Below are the top 10 stocks in QQQ that have led the charge on Wednesday. 

No. 10: The Trade Desk (NASDAQ:TTD) +17.4%.

No. 9: Marvell Technology (NASDAQ:MRVL) +17.7%.

No. 8: Palantir Technologies (NASDAQ:PLTR) +17.8%.

No. 7: Warner Bros. Discovery (NASDAQ:WBD) +17.9%.

No. 6: ON Semiconductor Corporation (NASDAQ:ON) +18.6%.

No. 5: Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) +19.1%.

No. 4: Advanced Micro Devices (NASDAQ:AMD) +20.1%.

No. 3: Arm Holdings (NASDAQ:ARM) +20.8%.

No. 2: Microchip Technology (NASDAQ:MCHP) +21.9%.

No. 1: MicroStrategy Incorporated (NASDAQ:MSTR) +23.2%.

Prices are as of 3:00 pm EST.

See additional ETFs that track both the Nasdaq 100 (NDX) and the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND): (NASDAQ:QQQ), (NASDAQ:QQQM), (NYSEARCA:QLD), (NASDAQ:TQQQ), (NASDAQ:SQQQ), and (NYSEARCA:QID).

data.title: Crypto prices surge after Trump halts tariffs for non-retaliating nations
data.description:
The crypto market jolted higher Wednesday after President Donald Trump announced a 90-day pause on tit-for-tat tariffs for more than 75 countries but a higher levy on Chinese imports.

Bitcoin's (BTC-USD) price bounced back to last week's levels above the $80K psychological technical level, surging 7.6% over the last 24 hours to $82K as of 2:55 p.m. ET. Earlier in the week, the highest-profile token failed several times in surpassing the $80K mark, trading as low as $74.6K.

Meanwhile, quite a few major altcoins outpaced BTC's intraday spike, including Ondo (ONDO-USD) +23%, Hedera Hashgraph (HBAR-USD) +18%, Sui (SUI-USD) +17%, Pepe (PEPE-USD) +15%, ChainLink (LINK-USD) +15% and Avalanche (AVAX-USD) +14%.

Stoking the gains, Trump announced a 90-day pause on counter tariffs for more than 75 countries that had reached out for negotiations, as well as a reduced 10% counter tariff rate for all countries but China during the span.

Crypto-related stocks shot up with the broader stock market on the news. Coinbase Global (COIN), the U.S.'s largest crypto exchange, soared 19%, Strategy (MSTR), the world's largest corporate bitcoin (BTC-USD) holder, gapped up 24%, and crypto asset manager Galaxy Digital (OTCPK:BRPHF) gained 21%. 

Bitcoin (BTC-USD) miners: MARA (MARA) +18%, Riot Platforms (RIOT) +11%, Bit Digital (BTBT) +4.9%, Core Scientific (CORZ) +14%, CleanSpark (CLSK) +14%, HIVE Digital Technologies (HIVE) +14%.

data.title: Core CPI inflation expected to tick up in March M/M, even before tariffs
data.description:
The U.S. Department of Labor will release March's consumer price index data on Thursday, what's normally a closely watched metric that provides insight on inflation. But this month, tariff drama is likely to overshadow interest in inflation trends.

"It feels like we're in a little bit of a different regime at the moment now, given the drama of what's going on," Brian Coulton, chief economist at Fitch Ratings, told Seeking Alpha in an interview. Indeed, on Wednesday, China announced that it's ratcheting up tariffs on U.S. goods to 84% from 34% in retaliation for the U.S.'s 104% tariff on Chinese goods. And Wednesday afternoon, President Donald Trump declared a 90-day pause on higher tariffs for more than 75 countries that sought to start trade negotiations with the U.S. 

"Particularly after the last 48 hours, with the tariff shock, has just moved to a level now that this is really looking like a major global macro shock," Coulton said. "Of course, it's a shock that's going to involve a lot more US inflation, as well."

Still, with data received so far, the U.S. economy goes into the shock on relatively solid footing. "There've been some signs of a slowdown, but the hard data is holding up a lot better than a lot of sentiment indicators and the markets [indicated]," he said. 

The March core CPI, which excludes the volatile food and energy categories, is expected to rise 0.3% in March, compared with the 0.2% increase in February, according to the consensus of economists. On a Y/Y basis, core CPI is expected to rise 3.0%, slowing from the 3.1% increase in the previous month.

The headline CPI number, which includes food and energy, is expected to edge up 0.1%, slowing from the 0.2% increase in February. On a Y/Y basis, that would come to +2.6% vs. +2.8% in the prior month.

Even before the tariff shock, inflation has stayed stubbornly above the Federal Reserve's 2% target. "A lot of it's about services inflation. A big chunk of that has been rental inflation, which has proved a lot stickier than the Fed and almost anybody expected," Coulton said.

Looking at three-month over three-month core CPI, on an annualized basis, inflation had made significant progress from mid-2024 until September. "And since September of last year, almost coinciding exactly when the Fed started cutting rates, it's gone back up again, and that I think has been a bit of a shock for the Fed," he added.

"The March CPI data will feel dated following President Trump's announcement of significantly larger tariffs across trading partners, but should shed some light on how the changing trade environment was already beginning to affect pricing," said Wells Fargo Senior Economist Sarah House in a note to clients.

The bank estimates that headline CPI was unchanged in March, leading the year-ago rate to sink to a six-month low of 2.5%. But the details are likely to be less encouraging as  "the drag from energy goods was fanned by growth concerns, core goods inflation was already on the upswing and services disinflation remains frustratingly slow."

Wells Fargo expects that core CPI rose 0.2% M/M, bringing the Y/Y growth to a four-year low of 3.0%. "March now looks set to mark the nadir in core inflation this year, however, as the administration's efforts to reorient U.S. trade lead to faster price growth," House wrote in a note to clients.

Citi's Veronica Clark expects a 0.22% increase in core CPI in March, translating to a 0.18% increase in core PCE. The Federal Reserve prefers to measure inflation through the core personal consumption expenditure ("PCE") price index, considering it a better gauge of underlying inflation. 

"While counterintuitive, we expect softer goods prices in March due to falling used car prices, although risks for goods prices are clearly tilted to the upside over the coming months due to substantial price increases from tariffs (although March may still be slightly soon to see these direct effects)," Citi's Clark wrote in a note to clients.

Evercore ISI's Stan Shipley expects core CPI to rise 0.31% M/M, while the Y/Y rate should drop. He expects headline CPI to be flat M/M as gasoline prices dropped sharply in March. "Tariffs, if they stay in place, should start to show up in the May/June CPI releases," he wrote in a note to clients.

In assessing incoming data, Shipley said "surprises in macro indicators that are associated with growth (employment, retail sales, industrial production...) will likely move the fixed income market more than surprises concerning inflation (CPI, PPI, average hourly earnings...)."

Fitch Ratings' Coulton expects tariffs to filter through in data that's released in the summer.

With three-month core CPI, on an annualized basis, rising in recent months, "I think this is not a comfortable starting point for the Fed going into this tariff shock," he said.

data.title: Constellation Energy raised to Buy at Citi, which says it's time to buy the dip
data.description:
Constellation Energy (NASDAQ:CEG) is upgraded to Buy from Hold with a $232 price target at Citi, saying the stock's risk/reward profile looks more attractive after the recent selloff, which saw shares chopped in half from January 24 to April 4.

Citi analyst Ryan Levine highlights several factors contributing to his upgrade, including the potential for co-location deals estimated to involve ~1 GW of uprates and 1.2 GW from existing operations, potential natural gas builds in Texas, a favorable stock issuance by Calpine, and downward protection from power price movements provided by production tax credits.

Levine said he believes Constellation (NASDAQ:CEG) can achieve the $232 PT without any behind-the-meter deals or unannounced growth, and any material change to AI power market policies would create material upside for the company.

Constellation Energy (CEG) shares, which had been trading around the flatline, +16.6% after President Trump announced a 90-day pause on tariffs for most of the world.

In the defensive regulated utility space, Levine reiterates his Buy rating for Consolidated Edison (ED), believing it is uniquely positioned because of a nuance in its earnings outlook, where 93% of 2026 earnings is tied to a financial ROE formula scheduled to be filed in May 2025 that is based on trailing three-month stock prices.

The weaker the peer stocks and the higher the market risk premium, the higher the expected earnings outlook for ConEd (ED), Levine says.

data.title: Pharma, MedTech stocks bounce back as Trump pauses tariffs
data.description:
Global drugmakers and MedTech companies bounced back after U.S. President Donald Trump issued a 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs for all countries except China, ending a key overhang for the healthcare sector.

While Pharma was immune from Trump’s sweeping tariff measures, which included MedTech last week, Wall Street was bracing for imminent impact given his repeated threats to subject pharmaceuticals to higher tariffs in an attempt to boost domestic production.

Global pharma stocks came under renewed selling pressure in the morning hours Wednesday after Trump threatened to impose a "major" tariff on all pharmaceutical imports “very shortly.”  “We’re going to tariff our pharmaceuticals, and once we do that, they are going to come rushing back into our country, because we’re the big market," he said at a dinner in Washington, D.C., on Tuesday.

With his tariff measures on hold for now, major drugmakers reversed early losses.

Notable outperformers include Viatris  (VTRS), Bausch Health (BHC), Teva Pharmaceutical (TEVA), Novo Nordisk (NVO), Gilead (GILD), Amgen (AMGN), Pfizer (PFE), Eli Lilly (LLY), Merck (MRK) and Vertex Pharma (VRTS). Veterinary drugmakers Elanco Animal Health (ELAN), and Zoetis (ZTS) were also among notable gainers.

VanEck Vectors Pharmaceutical ETF (NASDAQ:PPH), which represents 25 global pharma stocks, and SPDR S&P Pharmaceuticals ETF (NYSEARCA:XPH) ended a four-day selloff, as did Health Care SPDR ETF (NYSEARCA:XLV).

Meanwhile, iShares U.S. Medical Devices ETF (NYSEARCA:IHI) climbed ~5%, marking its best one-day since November 2022.

Notable gainers in the MedTech: Intuitive Surgical (ISRG), DexCom (DXCM), Baxter International (BAX), ResMed (RMD), Teleflex (TFX), Stryker (SYK), Boston Scientific (BSX), Edwards Lifesciences (EW), TransMedics (TMDX), Becton, Dickinson (BDX), Medtronic (MDT), Glaukos (GKOS).

data.title: WW International sinks on report it's preparing for bankruptcy filing
data.description:
WW International (NASDAQ:WW) plunged 50% on a report that it's preparing for a potential bankruptcy filing in the coming months in a plan that will give control of the business to its creditors.

WW, formerly known as Weight Watchers, is still in discussions with a major group of lenders and bondholders, according to a WSJ report on Wednesday, which cited people familiar with the matter.  

WW International (NASDAQ:WW) didn't immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's email request for comment. 

Bloomberg reported earlier this month that WW was in talks with its creditors to swap some of its debt for equity  and effectively hand over control of the company to its bondholders.

Shares of WW have plunged more than 75% over the past year as the entry of GLP-1 drugs such as Ozempic has hurt the company's sales.

data.title: Bessent's message to the world: 'do not retaliate, and you will be rewarded'
data.description:
U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent made some quick remarks shortly after President Donald Trump announced a 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs for more than 75 countries but a higher levy on Chinese imports.

His message to global trading partners: "do not retaliate, and you will be rewarded."

“We saw the successful negotiating strategy that President Trump implemented a week ago today,” Bessent said. "It has brought more than 75 countries forward to negotiate. It’s a great courage for him to stay the course until this moment, and what we have ended up with here, as I told you, is an escalation.”

This is a developing story. Check back for updates.

data.title: Travel stocks launched higher as recession odds dim
data.description:
Travel and leisure stocks were launched higher in Wednesday’s afternoon trade, riding the wave of optimism that President Trump’s decision to pause U.S. tariffs for 90 days will avert an economic recession.

Airlines and cruise operators are outperforming the rest of the travel sector with gains of 20% or more for names like Royal Caribbean (NYSE:RCL), United Airlines (NASDAQ:UAL), and Frontier Group Holdings (NASDAQ:ULCC) followed by outsized gains in the leisure category to include Six Flags Entertainment (NYSE:FUN), United Parks and Resorts (NYSE:PRKS), and Disney (NYSE:DIS).

Casino stocks are also ripping higher, with Bally’s Corp (NYSE:BALY) leading the pack with a 35% gain from Tuesday’s close. And despite an exposure to Macau (which remains under a heavy tariff burden) Wynn Resorts (NASDAQ:WYNN) is trading 12% higher, Caesar’s Entertainment (NASDAQ:CZR) up 12%, and Las Vegas Sands (NYSE:LVS) up by 8%.

Finally, among hotels and resorts, Hyatt Hotels (NYSE:H), Hilton Worldwide (NYSE:HLT), Wyndham Hotels & Resorts (NYSE:WH) and Marriott International (NASDAQ:MAR) are all currently trading with double-digit gains.

data.title: White House steps back from potential export curbs on Nvidia's H20 chips: report
data.description:
The Trump Administration is apparently pausing the implementation of an export ban to China for Nvidia's (NASDAQ:NVDA) H20 graphic processing units, according to a report by NPR.

The H20 is the most advanced chip offered by Nvidia that is still allowed to be exported to China. Industry observers and Chinese tech companies had expected the H20 to be added to the list of other banned hardware. Alibaba (BABA), Tencent (OTCPK:TCEHY) (OTCPK:TCTZF) and ByteDance (BDNCE) placed at least $16B in orders for Nvidia's H20 server chips in the first three months of 2025, ahead of a potential U.S. ban on the sale of the AI chip in China.

The H20 chips are slower in performance than Nvidia's new Blackwell chips, which the company sells in most markets outside China. 

Nvidia shares spiked 14% during early afternoon trading on Wednesday. 

The change of heart by the White House came after Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang attended a $1M per head dinner at Mar-a-Lago last week, according to the report by NPR. Nvidia allegedly said it would make new investments in U.S.-based AI data centers.

Nvidia declined to comment on the report.

Also on Wednesday, Trump issued a 90-day pause on most of the new tariffs that just went into effect after midnight. However, China was not exempted. Rather, Trump said he was raising tariffs there even further - to 125%.

"I have authorized a 90 day PAUSE, and a substantially lowered Reciprocal Tariff during this period, of 10%, also effective immediately," he added.

Semiconductor stocks across the board began rebounding on the news, many posting double-digit gains. Qualcomm (NASDAQ:QCOM) jumped 12%, Marvell Technology (NASDAQ:MRVL) and Qorvo (NASDAQ:QRVO) both surged 16% and Advanced Micro Devices (NASDAQ:AMD) rocketed 18%. Even Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) soared 17% higher. 

Not one name on Seeking Alpha's Top Semiconductor Stocks list was in the red.

The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index (SOX) surged a staggering 15%.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.
